source: Marking key analysis + model answer gap patterns

failures.description: Candidate lists facts without providing causal links
failures.example: Warm climates have riper fruit. (for an explain question)
failures.correction: State the mechanism: 'Higher temperatures accelerate sugar accumulation and phenolic ripening, producing wines with higher alcohol and riper fruit character.'

failures.description: Candidate gives one detailed point for a 4-mark question
failures.example: MLF softens wine acidity. (1 point only for 4-mark question)
failures.correction: MLF converts malic to lactic acid (1); reducing overall acidity (1); adding creamy/buttery texture (1); used for full-bodied white wines requiring less sharp acidity (1)

failures.description: Candidate uses sensory language outside the WSET SAT scale
failures.example: The wine tastes elegant and complex
failures.correction: Use scale terms: 'medium(+) intensity, clear secondary and tertiary complexity, well-balanced, long finish'

failures.failure: Quality level inconsistent with tasting notes
failures.description: Candidate selects Outstanding after noting simple/primary character
failures.example: Primary fruit only, short finish → 'Outstanding quality'
failures.correction: Simple wine with short finish = Acceptable quality

failures.description: Candidate completes quality but forgets readiness scale
failures.correction: Always conclude with both quality level AND readiness level for Unit 2

failures.description: Candidate assigns tertiary descriptors as primary (e.g. honey listed as primary aroma)
failures.correction: Honey is tertiary (bottle ageing); vanilla is secondary (oak); citrus is primary. Assign correctly to earn marks.

failures.description: Candidate attempts to identify tertiary aromas for a simple wine and loses time/marks on non-existent complexity
failures.correction: For simple wines: state 'simple' (1 mark) + 4–5 primary descriptors. Do not invent secondary or tertiary aromas.
